content:
# Exodus Chapter 7 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~34v-35r (PDF pp. 67-68); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 47-48 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_007_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_007_page_002.jpg`

---

**7:1** And YHWH said to Moses, "See, I have given you as a god to Pharaoh, and Aaron your brother shall be your prophet. [fn1]

**7:2** You shall speak all that I command you, and Aaron your brother shall speak to Pharaoh, and he shall send out the sons of Israel from his land.

**7:3** And I will make hard [qashah] the heart of Pharaoh, and I will multiply My signs and My wonders in the land of Egypt. [fn2]

**7:4** And Pharaoh will not listen to you, and I will lay My hand upon Egypt, and I will bring out My hosts, My people the sons of Israel, from the land of Egypt by great judgments. [fn3]

**7:5** And the Egyptians shall know that I am YHWH, when I stretch out My hand over Egypt and bring out the sons of Israel from their midst."

**7:6** And Moses and Aaron did as YHWH commanded them; so they did.

**7:7** And Moses was eighty years old, and Aaron was eighty-three years old, when they spoke to Pharaoh.

**7:8** And YHWH spoke to Moses and to Aaron, saying:

**7:9** "When Pharaoh speaks to you, saying, 'Give for yourselves a wonder,' then you shall say to Aaron, 'Take your staff and cast it before Pharaoh,' and it shall become a sea-serpent." [fn4]

**7:10** And Moses and Aaron went in to Pharaoh, and they did so, as YHWH had commanded. And Aaron cast his staff before Pharaoh and before his servants, and it became a sea-serpent.

**7:11** And Pharaoh also called the wise men and the sorcerers, and they also, the magicians of Egypt, did so with their secret arts.

**7:12** And each man cast down his staff, and they became sea-serpents. But the staff of Aaron swallowed their staffs. [fn5]

**7:13** And the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened [chazaq], and he did not listen to them, as YHWH had spoken. [fn6]

**7:14** And YHWH said to Moses, "The heart of Pharaoh is heavy [kaved]; he refuses to send out the people. [fn7]

**7:15** Go to Pharaoh in the morning -- behold, he goes out to the water -- and you shall station yourself to meet him upon the lip of the River, and the staff which was turned into a snake you shall take in your hand. [fn8]

**7:16** And you shall say to him, 'YHWH, the God of the Hebrews, has sent me to you, saying: Send out My people, that they may serve Me in the wilderness. And behold, you have not listened until now.

**7:17** Thus says YHWH: By this you shall know that I am YHWH -- behold, I am striking with the staff that is in My hand upon the waters that are in the River, and they shall be turned to blood.

**7:18** And the fish that are in the River shall die, and the River shall stink, and the Egyptians shall be weary of drinking water from the River.'"

**7:19** And YHWH said to Moses, "Say to Aaron, 'Take your staff and stretch out your hand over the waters of Egypt -- over their rivers, over their canals, over their pools, and over every gathering of their waters -- and they shall become blood.' And there shall be blood in all the land of Egypt, both in the wood and in the stone." [fn9]

**7:20** And Moses and Aaron did so, as YHWH had commanded. And he lifted the staff and struck the waters that were in the River, before the eyes of Pharaoh and before the eyes of his servants, and all the waters that were in the River were turned to blood.

**7:21** And the fish that were in the River died, and the River stank, and the Egyptians were not able to drink water from the River. And the blood was in all the land of Egypt.

**7:22** And the magicians of Egypt did so with their secret arts, and the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened [chazaq], and he did not listen to them, as YHWH had spoken. [fn6]

**7:23** And Pharaoh turned and went into his house, and he did not set his heart even to this. [fn10]

**7:24** And all the Egyptians dug around the River for water to drink, for they were not able to drink from the waters of the River.

**7:25** And seven days were fulfilled after YHWH struck the River.

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- "I have given you as a god to Pharaoh"** -- Hebrew: *netatikha elohim le-Far'oh* (נְתַתִּיךָ אֱלֹהִים לְפַרְעֹה). The verb *natan* ("to give, to set, to make") with the accusative *elohim* creates a startling declaration: Moses is placed in the divine role relative to Pharaoh, and Aaron functions as his *navi* ("prophet, spokesman") -- the parallel is precise: as a prophet speaks for God, so Aaron speaks for Moses-as-god. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders *dedoka se theon Pharao* ("I have given you as god to Pharao"). EMET renders "a god" (indefinite, literal), LOGOS renders "God" (capitalized, theological weight), and PNEUMA renders "like God" (comparative, interpretive). The consensus renders "a god" (lowercase, indefinite) because the Hebrew *elohim* here is anarthrous (no article) and functions predicatively: Moses does not *become* God but is *given as* a god-figure relative to Pharaoh. The lowercase preserves the functional rather than ontological sense: Moses stands in the role of the divine authority whom Pharaoh must obey. The LXX's *theon* (without article) supports the indefinite reading.

> **[fn2] v.3 -- "I will make hard [qashah]"** -- Hebrew: *va'ani aqsheh et-lev Par'oh* (וַאֲנִי אַקְשֶׁה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה). This is the hiphil imperfect of *qashah* (קשה), "to be hard, to make hard." This is the only occurrence of *qashah* in the entire Exodus hardening sequence, and it is spoken by God as a prospective declaration. SOPHIA renders "make hard" for *qashah*, following the established convention: *chazaq* = "strengthen" / "was strengthened"; *kaved* = "heavy" / "make heavy"; *qashah* = "make hard." PNEUMA's "harden" collapses the three distinct Hebrew roots into a single English concept, losing the semantic differentiation that the Hebrew text carefully maintains. EMET initially rendered *chazaq* but self-corrected to *qashah*. LOGOS renders "make hard." The LXX uses *skleruno* ("to harden"), which corresponds to *qashah*.

> **[fn3] v.4 -- "My hosts, My people the sons of Israel"** -- Hebrew: *tsiv'otai et-ammi benei-Yisra'el* (צִבְאֹתַי אֶת־עַמִּי בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל). YHWH calls the enslaved Israelites "My hosts" (*tseva'ot*) -- a military term for organized armies. PNEUMA renders "my armies -- my people, the Israelites," using "Israelites" rather than "sons of Israel." The SOPHIA convention follows the Hebrew *benei Yisra'el* as "sons of Israel" throughout. LOGOS renders "lay My hand upon" for *natati et-yadi be-* ("I will give My hand against"), which all three translators treat similarly; the consensus follows LOGOS's idiomatic rendering while preserving the prepositional force.

> **[fn4] vv.9-10, 12 -- *Tannin*: "sea-serpent"** -- Hebrew: *tannin* (תַּנִּין). This word is critically distinct from *nachash* (נָחָשׁ, "snake/serpent"), which was used for the staff-to-serpent transformation in 4:3. The *tannin* is the great sea-creature, chaos-monster, dragon of the deep -- it appears in Genesis 1:21 (*tanninim gedolim*, the great sea-creatures), Isaiah 27:1 (the cosmic serpent Leviathan), and Ezekiel 29:3 where Pharaoh himself is called "the great *tannin* who lies in the midst of his rivers." The choice of *tannin* before Pharaoh is deliberate: Aaron's staff becomes the very creature associated with Pharaoh's own mythological power, and then swallows the Egyptian replicas. The LXX confirms the distinction, rendering *drakon* (dragon) for *tannin* and *ophis* (snake) for *nachash*. EMET renders "sea-serpent/dragon" (with dual gloss); LOGOS renders "serpent" (flattening the distinction); PNEUMA renders "sea-serpent." The consensus follows PNEUMA's "sea-serpent" as the best single-word English rendering that preserves the distinction from *nachash* ("snake") while evoking the aquatic, mythological register. LOGOS's use of "serpent" for both *tannin* and *nachash* is the weakest rendering here, as it erases a distinction the Hebrew text insists upon.

> **[fn5] v.12 -- "The staff of Aaron swallowed their staffs"** -- Hebrew: *vayivla matteh-Aharon et-mattotam* (וַיִּבְלַע מַטֵּה־אַהֲרֹן אֶת־מַטֹּתָם). The Hebrew specifically says the *staff* swallowed, not the sea-serpent -- a detail all three translators preserve. The verb *bala'* ("to swallow, to engulf") is the same verb used when the earth swallows Korah's rebels (Numbers 16:32) and when the sea swallows the Egyptian army (Exodus 15:12). The LXX confirms: *katepien he rhabdos he Aaron* ("the staff of Aaron swallowed down"). The instrument of divine authority devours the rivals.

> **[fn6] vv.13, 22 -- *Chazaq*: "was strengthened"** -- Hebrew: *vayekhezaq lev Par'oh* (וַיֶּחֱזַק לֵב פַּרְעֹה). The qal of *chazaq*: Pharaoh's heart "was strengthened" -- it held firm, became resolute. The heart is the grammatical subject; no divine agent is named. This is the stative/intransitive use: Pharaoh's heart strengthened itself. The SOPHIA convention renders *chazaq* as "strengthen" / "was strengthened," distinct from "make hard" (*qashah*, v.3) and "heavy" (*kaved*, v.14). PNEUMA's "grew rigid" conflates *chazaq* with the semantic range of *qashah* (rigidity, hardness). The LXX uses *katischuo* ("to prevail, to be strong") in v.13, confirming the "strength" rather than "rigidity" sense, though interestingly switches to *skleruno* ("to harden") in v.22 -- an internal inconsistency in the Greek.

> **[fn7] v.14 -- *Kaved*: "heavy"** -- Hebrew: *kaved lev Par'oh* (כָּבֵד לֵב פַּרְעֹה). The adjective *kaved* ("heavy, weighty") from the root *k-b-d* describes Pharaoh's heart as sluggish, dull, unresponsive. The same root produces *kavod* ("glory, weight") -- an ironic connection between the glory Pharaoh claims and the heaviness that will destroy him. This is a divine description of an existing state, not an act of divine causation. The SOPHIA convention renders *kaved* as "heavy." PNEUMA's "unyielding" loses the specific somatic image of weight and heaviness, and encroaches on the semantic territory of *qashah* (hardness, rigidity). LOGOS renders "heavy," matching the consensus. The LXX uses *bareo* ("to weigh down"), confirming the weight-image.

> **[fn8] v.15 -- *Nachash* vs. *tannin*; "the River"** -- Hebrew: *ha-matteh asher nehpakh le-nachash* (הַמַּטֶּה אֲשֶׁר־נֶהְפַּךְ לְנָחָשׁ). Here the text reverts to *nachash* ("snake"), not *tannin*, referring back to the burning-bush transformation of 4:3. The staff in Moses' hand is identified by its original transformation, distinct from Aaron's staff which became a *tannin* before Pharaoh. The consensus renders "snake" for *nachash* here to sharpen the distinction from "sea-serpent" for *tannin*. The LXX confirms: *ophis* ("snake"), not *drakon*. -- The River: Hebrew *ha-Ye'or* (הַיְאֹר), from Egyptian *itrw/iarw*. EMET preserves the Egyptian loanword "Ye'or"; LOGOS and PNEUMA render "Nile." The SOPHIA convention capitalizes "the River" -- preserving the Hebrew's use of the definite article (*ha-Ye'or*, "the River") while signaling that this is not just any river but *the* River, Egypt's sacred waterway. "The River" avoids both the foreignness of "Ye'or" and the modern geographical specificity of "Nile" (a name from Greek *Neilos*, not from the Hebrew text). The phrase "upon the lip of the River" preserves the Hebrew *al-sfat ha-Ye'or*, where *safah* means "lip, edge, bank."

> **[fn9] v.19 -- "In the wood and in the stone"** -- Hebrew: *uva'etsim uva'avanim* (וּבָעֵצִים וּבָאֲבָנִים). Literally "and in the trees/wood and in the stones." The context implies water stored in wooden and stone vessels -- even reserves turned to blood, making the plague inescapable. LOGOS renders "in vessels of wood and in vessels of stone" (interpretive expansion, making the implied explicit). PNEUMA renders "even in vessels of wood and stone" (similar). EMET renders "in the trees and in the stones" (maximally literal). The consensus renders "in the wood and in the stone" -- a middle path that follows the Hebrew's bare nouns without adding the word "vessels" (which is not in the MT) while using the material sense of *'ets* and *'even* ("wood" and "stone" rather than "trees" and "stones"). The LXX renders *en tois xulois kai en tois lithois* ("in the wooden [things] and in the stones"), also without an explicit word for vessels.

> **[fn10] v.23 -- "He did not set his heart even to this"** -- Hebrew: *velo-shat libbo gam-la-zo't* (וְלֹא־שָׁת לִבּוֹ גַּם־לָזֹאת). The verb *shat* (from *shit*, "to set, to place") with *lev* ("heart") means "to direct one's attention, to take to heart." The particle *gam* ("even, also") intensifies the dismissal: not even *this* moved him. EMET renders "he did not set his heart also to this"; LOGOS: "he did not set his heart even to this"; PNEUMA: "he did not take even this to heart." The consensus follows LOGOS: "he did not set his heart even to this" -- preserving the Hebrew idiom *shat libbo* ("set his heart") while rendering *gam* as "even" (stronger than EMET's "also"). PNEUMA's "take to heart" is a natural English idiom but reverses the Hebrew syntax, placing "heart" as an object of the prepositional phrase rather than as the direct object of "set."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### 1. "A god" vs. "God" vs. "Like God" to Pharaoh (v. 1)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "a god" (indefinite, literal -- *netatikha elohim*)
- **LOGOS:** "God" (capitalized, theological weight)
- **PNEUMA:** "like God" (comparative, interpretive)

**SOPHIA's decision:** "a god" -- following EMET. The Hebrew *elohim* here is anarthrous and predicative: Moses is *given as* a god-figure to Pharaoh. The indefinite, lowercase rendering correctly conveys that Moses does not become God ontologically but functions in the divine role within the Pharaoh encounter. LOGOS's capitalized "God" risks implying an identification with the deity. PNEUMA's "like God" adds a comparative particle not present in the Hebrew -- the text does not say *ke-elohim* ("like God") but simply *elohim* ("a god"). The LXX's *theon* (without article) supports the indefinite reading. The force of the statement is not diminished by lowercase: to be "a god to Pharaoh" is to occupy the position of absolute authority that Pharaoh claimed for himself.

### 2. Qashah Rendering (v. 3)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "make hard" (self-corrected from initial "strengthen")
- **LOGOS:** "make hard"
- **PNEUMA:** "harden"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "make hard" -- following EMET (corrected) and LOGOS. See fn2 above. The SOPHIA hardening-verb convention requires distinct renderings for the three Hebrew roots. "Harden" is too generic and collapses the careful lexical differentiation the Hebrew maintains. The three roots describe three different experiences of resistance: *qashah* = rigidity, *chazaq* = strength/resolve, *kaved* = weight/dullness. "Make hard" captures the specific quality of *qashah*.

### 3. Tannin: "Sea-serpent" vs. "Serpent" (vv. 9, 10, 12)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "sea-serpent/dragon" (dual gloss)
- **LOGOS:** "serpent" (flattened, same word used for *nachash*)
- **PNEUMA:** "sea-serpent"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "sea-serpent" -- following PNEUMA. See fn4 above. LOGOS's rendering is the weakest here, as it erases the *tannin*/*nachash* distinction that the Hebrew text insists upon and the LXX confirms (*drakon* vs. *ophis*). The *tannin* is the chaos-creature of cosmic mythology, the creature Pharaoh himself is called in Ezekiel 29:3. "Sea-serpent" captures both the serpentine form and the aquatic/mythological dimension without requiring the dual-gloss apparatus that EMET uses.

### 4. Chazaq Rendering (vv. 13, 22)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "was strengthened"
- **LOGOS:** "was strengthened"
- **PNEUMA:** "grew rigid"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "was strengthened" -- following EMET and LOGOS. See fn6 above. PNEUMA's "grew rigid" conflates *chazaq* with *qashah* and introduces a quality (rigidity) that belongs to a different Hebrew root. The qal of *chazaq* denotes becoming strong, firm, resolute -- not rigid or brittle. The LXX's *katischuo* ("to prevail") confirms the strength-sense.

### 5. Kaved Rendering (v. 14)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "heavy"
- **LOGOS:** "heavy"
- **PNEUMA:** "unyielding"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "heavy" -- following EMET and LOGOS. See fn7 above. PNEUMA's "unyielding" is semantically defensible but loses the specific somatic image of weight. The root *k-b-d* is about heaviness, weightiness, dullness -- a heart that is too heavy to move, too sluggish to respond. "Unyielding" suggests active resistance, while "heavy" suggests passive inertia. The Hebrew distinction is important.

### 6. Nachash vs. Tannin in v. 15

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "nachash [snake]" -- preserves distinction from tannin
- **LOGOS:** "serpent" -- same word used for both tannin and nachash
- **PNEUMA:** "serpent" -- uses "serpent" here, "sea-serpent" for tannin

**SOPHIA's decision:** "snake" for *nachash* (v. 15), "sea-serpent" for *tannin* (vv. 9, 10, 12). The consensus uses two different English words to mirror the Hebrew's two different words. PNEUMA's "serpent" vs. "sea-serpent" almost achieves this but "serpent" and "sea-serpent" are too close; "snake" vs. "sea-serpent" creates a clearer distinction. LOGOS's use of "serpent" for both is inadequate. The LXX's *ophis* vs. *drakon* confirms the lexical distinction.

### 7. "In the wood and in the stone" vs. "Vessels of wood and stone" (v. 19)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "in the trees and in the stones" (maximally literal)
- **LOGOS:** "in vessels of wood and in vessels of stone" (interpretive expansion)
- **PNEUMA:** "even in vessels of wood and stone" (interpretive expansion)

**SOPHIA's decision:** "in the wood and in the stone" -- a middle path. See fn9. The Hebrew has bare nouns *'etsim* and *avanim* without any word for "vessels." The consensus does not add what the Hebrew omits, but renders the nouns as materials ("wood," "stone") rather than objects ("trees," "stones"), since the context implies containers, not natural features. The LXX similarly omits an explicit word for vessels.

### 8. "Set his heart" Idiom (v. 23)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "did not set his heart also to this"
- **LOGOS:** "did not set his heart even to this"
- **PNEUMA:** "did not take even this to heart"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "did not set his heart even to this" -- following LOGOS. The Hebrew idiom *shat libbo* ("set his heart") is preserved rather than replaced with the English idiom "take to heart." The word *gam* is rendered "even" (stronger than EMET's "also"). See fn10.

### 9. The River (throughout)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "Ye'or" (Egyptian loanword transliterated)
- **LOGOS:** "Nile" (modern English name)
- **PNEUMA:** "Nile" (modern English name)

**SOPHIA's decision:** "the River" (capitalized) -- following the established SOPHIA convention. See fn8. The Hebrew *ha-Ye'or* uses the definite article with a common noun that had become a proper designation for the Nile. "The River" mirrors this usage: a common noun elevated by context and capitalization into a proper name. This avoids the foreignness of "Ye'or" (EMET) and the anachronistic specificity of "Nile" (LOGOS, PNEUMA).

### 10. Divine Name: YHWH (throughout)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** YHWH
- **LOGOS:** "the LORD"
- **PNEUMA:** YHWH

**SOPHIA's decision:** YHWH -- following the established SOPHIA convention. LOGOS is the sole dissenter, as in previous chapters.

---

### Hardening-Verb Tracking -- Exodus 7

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Form | SOPHIA Rendering | Agent | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7:3 | qashah (קשה) | Hiphil imperfect *aqsheh* | "I will make hard" | YHWH (prospective) | Only occurrence of qashah in the hardening cycle; programmatic announcement |
| 7:13 | chazaq (חזק) | Qal *vayekhezaq* | "was strengthened" | Pharaoh's heart (self) | No divine agent named; heart as grammatical subject |
| 7:14 | kaved (כבד) | Adjective *kaved* | "is heavy" | Pharaoh's heart (state) | Divine diagnosis of existing condition; not an act of causation |
| 7:22 | chazaq (חזק) | Qal *vayekhezaq* | "was strengthened" | Pharaoh's heart (self) | Identical to v.13; follows magicians' replication |

*Pattern note: In chapter 7, all three hardening roots appear. God announces He will cause hardening (qashah, v.3), but the narrative descriptions present Pharaoh's heart as strengthening on its own (chazaq, vv. 13, 22) or as already in a heavy state (kaved, v. 14). The explicit divine hardening with God as grammatical agent does not begin until later in the plague cycle. The progression is theologically significant: Pharaoh's resistance begins as self-generated before it becomes divinely confirmed.*

### Cumulative Hardening-Verb Tracker (Exodus 4-7)

| Verse | Root | SOPHIA Rendering | Agent |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4:21 | chazaq | "strengthen" | YHWH (prospective) |
| 7:3 | qashah | "make hard" | YHWH (prospective) |
| 7:13 | chazaq | "was strengthened" | Heart (self) |
| 7:14 | kaved | "heavy" | Heart (state) |
| 7:22 | chazaq | "was strengthened" | Heart (self) |

---

*Consensus completed for Exodus 7:1-25 (25 verses). All readings follow the Leningrad Codex. LXX variants noted where significant. The first plague (blood) is rendered with "the River" throughout for the Nile. The tannin/nachash distinction is preserved as "sea-serpent" vs. "snake." The three hardening verbs are rendered distinctly: qashah = "make hard," chazaq = "strengthen" / "was strengthened," kaved = "heavy." The chapter opens with Moses' commissioning as "a god to Pharaoh" and closes with Pharaoh retreating into his house, refusing even to set his heart to the devastation.*

--- SOPHIA
